digest.intro: Two chokepoints, a functional cure signal, and platforms cracking open.

items.title: Two-Chokepoint Crisis: US Strikes Iran, Houthis Threaten Bab el-Mandeb
items.blurb: The structural story beneath the daily strike updates: the Iran conflict has simultaneously put the Strait of Hormuz and the Bab el-Mandeb under threat — the two chokepoints that together handle the majority of global oil exports and container shipping. A closure of both at once would be without modern precedent, reshaping energy prices and supply chains in ways that dwarf any single military exchange.

items.title: FCC to Repeal the 39% TV Ownership Cap
items.blurb: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr already granted Nexstar a waiver to exceed 50% of TV households — now he's announcing full repeal of the Congressional cap and replacing it with case-by-case review, published as an op-ed on Breitbart. The constitutional fight here is real: the FCC is asserting it can nullify a limit Congress explicitly set, which sets up an institutional clash that goes well beyond broadcast media.

items.title: Stripe and Advent Make Joint Offer to Acquire PayPal for $53B+
items.blurb: If this closes, Stripe — the payments infrastructure that most of the internet quietly runs on — absorbs PayPal's 400 million consumer accounts, collapsing the two dominant consumer payment rails under one design sensibility and one API. That's not just a finance story; it's a question of who writes the UX grammar for how money moves on the web.

items.title: Gene-Edited Islets: One T1D Patient, 14+ Months, No Immunosuppressants
items.blurb: Sana Biotechnology's gene-edited donor islets — engineered to evade the immune system — have kept a single Type 1 diabetes patient producing their own insulin for over 14 months post-transplant, without any immunosuppressant drugs. That last part is the mechanism worth watching: the historical barrier to islet transplants has always been lifelong immunosuppression, not the islets themselves. One patient, early data, but the biology is doing something it wasn't supposed to be able to do.

items.title: Therapeutic Peptides Against the 12 Hallmarks of Aging — Frontiers Review
items.blurb: A peer-reviewed narrative review sweeping PubMed and Scopus through January 2026 maps nine peptides — tirzepatide, epitalon, GHK-Cu, BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, CJC-1295, ipamorelin, bremelanotide — against the established 12 hallmarks of aging framework. It's the most rigorous primary reference available in this space right now, and it draws a sharp line between FDA-approved agents with robust trial data and the non-approved peptides where the evidence is still preclinical or early-phase.

items.title: Third-Party App Stores Are Coming to Google Play Next Week
items.blurb: The Epic-Google settlement is dead, which means Judge Donato's full antitrust remedies kick in — including Google being required to host rival app stores directly inside Google Play. This is a structurally different outcome than Apple's case, where Epic got almost nothing; here the platform is being forced to distribute its own competition. Indie developers and alternative ecosystems now have a real distribution path on Android that didn't exist a month ago.

items.title: The Lost Joy of Music Piracy
items.blurb: Rob Sheridan — the Nine Inch Nails creative director who also accidentally created the dancing baby meme — anchors this essay on what What.CD and Oink actually were: not just illegal file sharing but elaborate social ecosystems where scarcity, curation, and community created taste in ways Spotify's frictionless abundance structurally cannot. The argument is that the constraint was the culture, and removing it didn't liberate music discovery — it dissolved it.

items.title: Archie G. Norcross' Maine Forest Fire Maps (1918–22)
items.blurb: These circular maps were designed for fire tower lookouts — the geometry of the map literally mirrors the geometry of a human body rotating 360 degrees in place, so the tool fits the body fits the landscape. Kerouac spent a summer in one of these towers (broke, pre-On The Road, chasing Gary Snyder's ascetic ideal) and the essay uses that as a way into what it meant to be a person whose entire job was vigilant looking. Information design as embodied instrument, from 1918.

items.title: Applied Science: Cross-Sectioning Insects With a Femtosecond Laser Inside an Electron Microscope
items.blurb: Applied Science uses a femtosecond laser — pulses so short they ablate material before heat can diffuse — as a surgical scalpel to cross-section insects mid-body, then images the exposed internal structures with a scanning electron microscope. This channel sits at the exact intersection of obsessive craft and unreasonable technical depth; the combination of imaging modalities here (ultrafast laser + SEM) is something most university labs don't have running together.

facts.fact: FCC Chairman Carr granted Nexstar a waiver to reach over 50% of US TV households before formally announcing the repeal of the 39% cap — meaning the rule was already being treated as optional months before any vote.

Thinking Machines' Inkling model has 975 billion total parameters but only 41 billion active at inference time — a Mixture-of-Experts architecture trained on 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video, released as fully open weights.

The Ọṣun-Òṣogbo Sacred Grove in Nigeria — where a community of artists began erecting large-scale sculptures honoring the Yorùbá river goddess in the 1960s — spans 190 acres and is a UNESCO World Heritage site where the art environment is still actively maintained and expanded.

Ana Mendieta's Tate Modern retrospective — the UK's first major survey in over a decade — features restaged installations including candles lit live in the gallery, attempting to resurrect work that was by design ephemeral and unrepeatable.

NEPA's environmental impact statement provision — now responsible for multi-year, thousand-page review processes that have shaped environmental law worldwide — received no coverage in any major media publication when the law passed and 'neither debate, nor opposition, nor affirmative endorsement' in Congress.

art_picks.title: Decasia
art_picks.creator: Bill Morrison
art_picks.why: Nitrate film stock dissolving in real time — the decay IS the image, not a flaw in it. Morrison found footage of the physical substrate of cinema collapsing into abstract forms; it's a film about entropy as generative system, and it will make you want to build something that thinks about material degradation the way WebGL thinks about light.

art_picks.title: Haim Steinbach: once again the world is flat
art_picks.creator: Haim Steinbach
art_picks.why: Steinbach spent decades arranging commercial objects on formica shelves and calling it sculpture — which sounds thin until you realize he was building an exact anthropology of desire, status, and the invisible grammar that makes certain objects feel sacred. The work is about the systems underneath consumer culture, made visible by the obsessive neutrality of the gesture.

art_picks.title: Big Science
art_picks.creator: Laurie Anderson
art_picks.why: The 1982 album that turned spoken language, vocoder mythology, and field recording into a coherent emotional world — art functioning as community ritual, technology as feeling, self as construction project. 'O Superman' alone is a complete theory of infrastructure and longing compressed into eight minutes.

events.name: time is a flat circle — bitforms gallery
events.venue: bitforms gallery, 131 Allen St, Lower East Side
events.why: Exactly your world: four artists using digital images, networked infrastructure, and fiber-optic systems to explore how contemporary mythology is constructed and circulated — diaspora, colonial telecom history, and AI as carriers of inherited memory, at the gallery that has anchored new-media art in NYC for 25 years.

events.name: Architects of Liberation: Modernism in Western Africa — MoMA
events.why: Post-independence West African nations used architecture to project new images of civic identity — this show recovers the buildings, drawings, and architects that gave form to a region in transition, most shown publicly for the first time, rewriting the modernist canon from outside Europe.

events.name: Mavis Staples / Brother Wallace — SummerStage 40th Anniversary
events.why: Tonight, free, outdoors — an 86-year-old hall-of-famer (blues, rock, gospel) performing alongside her new album and opening act Brother Wallace; this is American music as living ritual, not nostalgia, and it's the kind of thing that only exists in NYC in summer.

events.name: Made in America: The Industrial Photography of Christopher Payne — Cooper Hewitt
events.venue: Cooper Hewitt, Smithsonian Design Museum, Upper East Side
events.why: 70+ large-format photographs from a decade-long obsessive project inside American factories — Payne went unreasonably deep on the hidden systems and craft of industrial making, and the Cooper Hewitt frames it as design history rather than documentary, which makes the systems legible.

events.name: De La Soul / Pete Rock — SummerStage
events.why: Two of the most sample-architecture-obsessed acts in hip-hop history on the same free outdoor bill — this is music as layered systems thinking, and a rare chance to hear Pete Rock's loop craft in the open air.
